data.title: Google Ups Privacy And Intelligence Ante With Latest Android Updates
data.description:
Beauty, as they say, is in the eye of the beholder. In the latest updated versions of Google's (NASDAQ:GOOG, NASDAQ:GOOGL) Android 16 and Wear 6 operating systems, for many, undoubtedly, the beauty will be found in the new Material 3 Expressive design language. It is, after all, a dramatic and attractive change versus previous user interface designs from Google. Plus, the level of detail on things, like how well the elements of the screen automatically adjust to your interactions in an intuitive and intelligent way, is definitely impressive.

However, the true inner beauty of the new OS stems from the under-the-hood changes that Google has integrated into the Android experience - some of which will be arriving very shortly. In particular, Google has integrated the intelligence of its generative AI-powered Gemini assistant throughout all its latest Android iterations, and it has incorporated an extensive range of security and privacy-related features into Android 16 as well.

To be fair, the Gemini integration had already started with Android 15 on a few select devices, but Android 16 expands that to all Android phones and tablets - without a subscription - and it extends the experience to smart watches running Wear OS 6, the latest version of Android Auto, and even Google smart TVs running Android.

On all these devices, you’ll soon be able to have the kind of voice-based natural language interactions that LLM-powered chatbots have started to get us all accustomed to. In environments like the car or in active situations when you’re wearing a smart watch, that can make a big difference in improving the usability of the overall Android experience. The addition of support for Gemini Live also means you can engage in lengthy conversations on virtually any topic while in those settings. Furthermore, few people who watch TV simultaneously have access to a keyboard, so accurate responses to voice-based queries are important in these situations as well.

The latest version of Gemini in Android 16 is bringing in visual capabilities, meaning Gemini can see and react to what’s on your device’s screen or what it sees through the camera. Most people have just begun to experiment with these kinds of multi-modal interactions, and they’re bound to be blown away by what the latest AI-powered assistant technology can do. Google even briefly hinted at bringing these capabilities to Android XR mixed reality devices, which it’s going to be talking about in more detail at next week’s Google I/O event.

On top of the intelligence features - which stand in stark contrast to where Apple (AAPL) currently finds itself with Siri and Apple Intelligence - Google also made a surprisingly broad range of announcements related to privacy and security for Android 16. From scam detection and personal identity authentication to mobile theft protection and other anti-fraud capabilities, the latest version of Android has an impressive array of new tools designed to keep people and their devices safer.

While they’re certainly not as pretty as a new user interface, these kinds of features offer a compelling set of deterrents that the huge number of people whose lives have become utterly dependent on their devices should find very attractive. The unfortunate truth is that the type, number and sophistication levels of scamming and other privacy-related attacks targeting people’s smartphones has grown extensively over the last few years. As a result, these types of privacy and security capabilities have quickly migrated from a nice-to-have to an absolute essential, and it’s great to see Google stepping up here.

For example, Google can now identify potential fraud in text messages and even during live phone calls to prevent bad actors from being able to surreptitiously take over a device or steal data. These features can also save people from unwittingly changing settings or sharing data that could let their devices or information be compromised. Real-time app scanning can also stop certain threats from being started from sideloading or remote loading of applications. A new Identity Check feature, first available for Google Pixel and Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) devices, offers stronger protection even in the event a PIN or password is compromised along with the phone being lost or stolen.

Google also expanded its little-known Advanced Protection capabilities in Android 16, which are designed for the most at-risk or security-conscious individuals, including public figures, journalists, and others who want to be shielded from today’s most sophisticated digital threats. New features including automatic intrusion logging of potential security events for further analysis, disabling auto-reconnect to insecure networks, securing USB ports from physical attacks, and more. All of these are on top of the existing protections in Advanced Protection, which offers an inter-connected group of security settings that can be easily turned on at once.

Finally, Google upgraded its my Find My Device service to what it’s calling Find Hub. In addition to the traditional capabilities for tracking lost devices and smart tags, Find Hub adds the ability to track the location of friends and family (with their consent and with the appropriate privacy-related settings to avoid unrecognized tracking). The company also announced upcoming support for smart tags with Ultra-Wideband (UWB) technology, satellite-based tracking and new types of embedded tags in things like skis, luggage and more.

All told, it’s a comprehensive set of updates that demonstrates Google’s continued moves to step outside the shadow of Apple and iOS, particularly here in the US market. After years of being seen as a laggard in many types of technologies and user interface, as well as thought of as more prone to security-related concerns, the realization of announcements like these will make Android a clearly viable and arguably even more advanced alternative to the Apple hegemony. The new capabilities of Gemini, in particular, bring a level of AI-powered intelligence that can really make the modern Android experience an impressive one. Toss in the ability to consistently use Gemini across a huge range of devices, and the story becomes even more compelling.

Disclaimer: Some of the author's clients are vendors in the tech industry.

Disclosure: None.

Original Source: Author

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Farmland Partners: Outlook Increased As Company Diversifies Revenue Streams
data.description:
Introduction

So far in 2025, Farmland Partners Inc. (NYSE:FPI) has underperformed the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ), delivering a loss of ~11%:

I previously covered Farmland Partners back in December 2024 with a Hold rating, arguing that despite an increase in AFFO (driven by deleveraging and cost savings), the AFFO multiple remained too high relative to other commercial REITs. Recent underperformance justifies my view, although I would note that the decline appears excessive in light of the surge in the SPDR Gold Shares ETF (GLD), which is up ~24% so far this year. I continue to believe that farmland should offer stronger long-term returns relative to gold thanks to its current cash flows and safe-haven status.

In this article, I share my highlights from the REIT's recently released Q1 2025 results. I confirm my Hold rating for the shares as the company's valuation remains quite high relative to both farmland peers, as well as the broader commercial REIT universe.

Company Overview

You can access all FPI results on its website here. Farmland Partners is a farmland REIT that owns 92,415 acres, primarily in the Corn Belt (46% of the portfolio) and the High Plains (23%). In addition, the company manages 46,751 acres for third parties:

60% of the REIT's land is used to grow primary crops (corn, soybean, wheat, rice, cotton), while 40% is devoted to specialty crops (citrus, avocados, tree nuts).

To increase returns, the company has also recently been increasing its lending to farmers, with loans and financing receivables accounting for 7% of the REIT's total assets.

The REIT's enterprise value totals $770 million, of which 64% is funded with common equity, 13% with preferred shares, and 23% with net debt.

Q1 2025 Results Overview

Farmland Partners reported AFFO of $0.05/share, down 16.7% Y/Y, driven in large part by a decline in net operating income /NOI/ by 16% Y/Y. This was due to lower property operating revenues (down 14.5% Y/Y) as the REIT has been downsizing its portfolio in recent quarters:

Even so, the company increased its full-year 2025 outlook to $0.28-0.34/share. The improved outlook is the result of higher interest income and larger solar, wind, recreation rent. Recent share repurchases also contributed to the higher AFFO per share:

Conference call highlights

The most bullish factor for FPI remains the discount to net asset value (NAV) of the portfolio, with management reiterating their rationale for share repurchases at current levels:

NAV of the portfolio, I haven't directly addressed this in the last couple of conference calls, so a couple people asked me so I'll do it here. We would say that the portfolio today is in the mid-14s, just kind of connecting the dots and to explain a little bit why we quit talking about it.

When we look our – at the gap between the NAV, as Paul indicated, and our stock price, we continue to believe that our own stock is effectively the cheapest farmland we can buy, and we will probably continue to do so as appropriate and as it makes sense.

I believe the discount to net asset value can largely be explained by the need to compensate for administrative overhead. Case in point, in 2025 the REIT is set to spend almost $12 million on general and administrative/legal expenses.

Another point worth mentioning is that the REIT is boosting its lending in a bid to grow AFFO:

What that means is we have shifted some of our investment to making a few more loans as opposed to property purchases, because the loans of course give us quite a bit higher current yield than owning a farm.

Company valuation

Farmland Partners trades at 33.5x its 2025 AFFO midpoint, one of the highest AFFO multiples you can find for a commercial REIT. Of course, this is the result of the company's modest leverage, with 64% of enterprise value funded with common equity.

Turning to enterprise-level cash flows, the company's Q1 2025 report shows that there are 99,000 preferred shares outstanding, carrying an interest rate of 3%. Annual interest due to the preferred shares is $3 million. Together with the $10.3 million in interest expenses and circa $14.6 million in AFFO, we see that cumulative cash flows to enterprise value total roughly $28 million.

Relative to the $770 million enterprise value, we can calculate that the REIT's market-implied cap rate stands at about 3.6%, which is quite low for a farmland REIT. For reference, Gladstone Land (LAND), which I covered recently, offers a market-implied cap rate of about 5%.

All in all, I still believe it will be quite hard for FPI to outperform VNQ over a long period of time. This is principally the result of a low starting market-implied cap rate and a NOI growth outlook close to inflation. As such, I confirm my Hold rating on FPI.

Risks

Recent land sales have reduced Farmland Partners' debt, and the company remains well-positioned to take advantage of the weakness in its share price via share repurchases. That said, I should note that the preferred shares are eligible for conversion starting in February 2026, which may have a dilutive impact on NAV upside. You can think about it this way - currently, the REIT has 47.2 million shares, which are eligible to capture upside of roughly 39% should the REIT reach its NAV of around $14.5/share. This represents a fair market value for the common equity of $685 million.

If the preferred shares are converted to common shares, shares outstanding will increase by roughly 9.6 million, reaching 56.8 million. In this scenario, the fair market value for the common equity would indeed jump to $785 million, but on a per-share basis, NAV will slump to roughly $13.80/share. Hence, the upside relative to the current share price will dip to only 33%. The company may address this risk by redeeming more preferred stock.

Farmland Partners may also be affected by ongoing trade turmoil, given the surplus of agricultural exports the United States enjoys with the rest of the world. This may result in difficulties for farmers to pay rent, as well as credit losses on the REIT's growing loan portfolio.

Conclusion

Farmland Partners reported weaker AFFO in Q1 2025 but boosted its full-year outlook, driven by non-traditional revenue sources such as lending and solar, wind, recreation rent, as well as ongoing share repurchases.

The company's valuation in terms of AFFO multiple has improved following recent land sales but remains well in excess of other REITs held in the VNQ ETF. As such, I continue to believe that over the long term, it will be quite challenging for FPI to meaningfully outperform the VNQ ETF.

Nevertheless, the company remains appealing for investors putting an emphasis on safety and capital preservation. Thus, I confirm a Hold rating for the shares.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: The Gap: Turnaround Progress Matters More Than Industry Turmoil
data.description:
The Gap, Inc. (NYSE:GAP) is looking to report the apparel company’s fiscal Q1 results for the February-April period on the 29th of May after market close. The quarter is set to continue Gap’s brand reinvigoration based on Old Navy’s and Gap’s brand relevance and continued margin expansion. On the other hand, the macroeconomic environment casts a shadow on Gap alongside the industry, causing pressure on the outlook.

In my previous October 2024 article on the stock, “The Gap: Looking For A Turnaround Continuation In Q3,” I upgraded my rating on Gap to Buy due to company’s underappreciated turnaround momentum. The stock has since returned 19%, comparing to S&P 500’s (SP500) return of 1%.

Gap’s Turnaround Should Continue in Q1, But the Industry Environment Is Turbulent

Gap’s turnaround has progressed; FY2024 showed stable but slow 1.3% sales growth with better comparable store sales growth of 3%, and brand progress expanded Gap’s operating margin by a significant 360 basis points to 7.4% through gross margin expansion and leverage in operating expenses. The fourth quarter still showed similarly good trends in comparable sales, while gross margin progress has now slowed down.

The upcoming Q1 report should continue similar underlying progress, where the focus is still mainly on Gap’s brand reinvigoration. Notably, on the brand relevance front, Google searches suggest quite a stable year-on-year performance for Old Navy in Q1, a notable improvement in relevance for Gap, but some weakness for the Banana Republic brand and a choppy performance for Athleta. As the two clearly largest brands are performing well, together responsible for 78% of total sales, the total brand outlook is relatively good for the quarter. The company’s Q4 earnings call remarks suggest a similar split between brands, also continuing FY2024 trends.

Operational improvements should continue to increase margins structurally. Gap set quite a slow FY2025 revenue growth outlook of 1-2% but operating income is expected to grow significantly faster, by 8-10%. Gap continues to expect slight further expansion in the gross margin and some leverage in SG&A based on tight expense management, resulting in $150 million in expected cost savings. Gap plans to close 35 of its 3569 outstanding stores during FY2025, contributing to the relatively slow sales growth outlook.

Wall Street’s expectations for Q1 are set at a reflecting level. Revenues are expected at $3.41 billion and the adjusted EPS at $0.44, suggesting 0.8% revenue growth and a $0.02 improvement in the EPS.

Negatively, the quarter’s industry outlook is now cast with shadows. A cold start to February caused slow sales for a while as Gap noted in the Q4 earnings call, causing management to expect in “flat to up slightly” sales for Q1 in the early March report. More notably, since, macroeconomic uncertainty has surged alongside tariff policies, causing a significant decline in consumer confidence in March and especially in April. The macroeconomic uncertainty is likely to pressure Gap’s Q1 below management’s previous expectations and below Wall Street’s consensus.

Representatively of the industry's struggles, American Eagle Outfitters (AEO) withdrew its FY2025 guidance on the 13th of May due to macroeconomic uncertainty.

Past Q1, the outlook remains quite cloudy for the industry. While the U.S. and China have reached a trade deal and reciprocal tariffs are on hold, alleviating some consumer uncertainty, consumers’ reaction is challenging to forecast after the previous decline in sentiment. The 10% baseline tariff will cause some cost pressure on sourcing, but apparel companies should be able to shift such a tariff into prices without too significant consumer resistance over the longer term. Gap’s guidance may also see some pressure in the Q1 report.

Ultimately, I believe that industry-level weakness is already priced in for the report, and Gap's solid brand performance should set Gap up to perform better than many industry peers - the underlying turnaround momentum remains strong. A weaker sales environment is likely to cause short-term earnings pressure, but Gap's long-term prospects are increasingly healthy regardless. The consumer sentiment will eventually rebound, and until then, Gap's healthy cash flow and balance sheet alleviate concern.

Reciprocal tariffs could add significantly more pressure, but the tariffs' imposition is highly uncertain as trade negotiations continue. Gap’s sourcing also seems to be relatively well-diversified, still setting up Gap well for supply chain adaptation if the currently on-hold reciprocal tariffs are ultimately imposed. The company sourced 27% of purchases from Vietnam and 19% of purchases from Indonesia as the two largest sourcing countries in FY2024, facing 46% and 32% reciprocal tariffs respectively.

There’s Still a Gap to Fair Value

I slightly updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Gap. I estimate more cautious growth given the current macroeconomic climate, including 0% revenue growth for FY2025 and stable 2% growth afterwards. Based on continued cost savings and slight gross margin expansion, I now estimate the EBIT margin to reach an 8% level from FY2026 forward, up from 7.4% in FY2024. I estimate a FY2025 EBIT performance below the current guidance due to macroeconomic uncertainty.

Gap’s cash flow conversion should remain good.

The estimates put Gap’s fair value at $36.14, 42% above the stock price at the time of writing. The stock remains attractively valued as the market’s sentiment lags Gap’s significant earnings turnaround.

The fair value estimate is up from $31.61 previously due to higher margin expectations.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 9.28%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a 5.84% interest rate, annualized from FY2024 interest expenses and debt. I again estimate a moderate 15% long-term debt-to-equity ratio.

For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.48% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.33% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the U.S. Seeking Alpha now estimates Gap’s beta at 1.21. With a liquidity premium of 0.3%, the cost of equity stands at 10.02% and the WACC at 9.28%.

Takeaway

Gap’s Q1 should show continued momentum from Old Navy and the Gap brand, while Banana Republic’s and Athleta’s sales performances are still likely to be quite muted. At the same time, operating efficiencies should continue to raise margins. An uncertain macroeconomic environment weighs on Gap and reciprocal tariffs pose a further risk, but The Gap, Inc.’s underlying momentum should still remain good through improved brand relevance. As the market’s sentiment continues to lag, I remain with a Buy rating for Gap.

data.title: Amazon: The Real Pain Is Still Coming
data.description:
After the results of the presidential elections in November were announced, we published a bearish article on Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN), in which we argued that the company is about to witness a rise in macro risks under the Trump administration. That’s exactly what has happened since that time as we’re now in the middle of a full-blown global trade war that’s not over yet.

While currently Amazon’s stock trades at a similar price at which it was trading last year when our article was published, we still believe that it has room to fall since not all risks have materialized yet. That is why Amazon remains a SELL for us.

This Is Not Looking Good

Although Amazon reported fairly good Q1 earnings results last month, primarily because the first quarter ended before any major tariffs were implemented by the Trump administration, its outlook was fairly weak. Its Q2 operating income guidance was below the overall expectations, which indicates that its bottom-line performance might suffer as a result of tariffs primarily on Chinese-made goods on which the company’s eCommerce platform greatly relies to generate profits.

That’s why it’s not a surprise that in recent months, there have been countless downward revenue revisions for Amazon primarily as a result of the trade war launched by President Trump last month. While in the last few days, there has been a de-escalation of the Sino-American trade war, the United States still imposes a 30% tariff on most China-made goods, and a 54% tariff on small shipments from China.

This is still a big issue for Amazon considering that the company itself in the latest quarterly report highlighted that China-based sellers account for a significant portion of its third-party seller services and advertising revenues. The company also noted in that report that a rise in any trade-related risks could adversely affect its performance. Considering that most products on Amazon are sourced from China, the company’s performance could indeed suffer in the following months while relatively high tariffs are still in place.

While Amazon’s CEO Andy Jassy said that sellers will likely pass costs on to consumers and there have already been reports from last month that the Chinese sellers in particular are planning price hikes on the eCommerce platform, there’s a possibility that it won’t help improve the overall sales.

Since the U.S. consumer sentiment is already at historically low levels and the American economy is expected to grow at a relatively low rate, there’s a risk that the price hikes won’t save the day.

Also, while Amazon’s eCommerce business will likely suffer as a result of tariffs, its cloud business might underperform as well in the upcoming quarters. At the beginning of this month, there were reports that both Microsoft (MSFT) and Amazon have paused some data center projects, which might signal that the demand is not as high as previously expected. If that’s the case, then increasing CapEx from$83 billion in 2024 to around$100 billion in 2025 primarily on AI projects might backfire and yield little return on investment this year.

It’s Not As Bad As It Seems?

Some upside for Amazon still remains. While the tariffs are unlikely to be fully eliminated, a much lower rate might nevertheless make it manageable for Amazon and its Chinese-based sellers to perform relatively well in the end.

The Trump administration is also looking to sign new trade deals with major nations in the following months. They have already signed a new trade deal with the United Kingdom earlier this month. If more such deals are signed, then the risks to the global economy might be minimized and make it possible for Amazon and its peers to perform better than expected over time.

The Real Value of Amazon

Right after the presidential elections in November ended, we created Amazon’s valuation model, which showed that the company’s intrinsic value was $116.39 per share. We understood that the trade war would have a major impact on Amazon’s business, but we didn’t expect the tariffs to be significantly increased so fast. Now that the tariffs are implemented and the global trade war is still ongoing despite the recent de-escalation, we decided to update our model.

The model is updated at a time when Amazon trades at $212.59 per share, its cash and total debt data has been taken from the latest earnings report, and the perpetual growth rate in the model remains at 3%.

The discount rate in the updated model is 8.30%. We arrived at that rate by calculating Amazon’s cost of debt using primarily the TTM numbers, and by figuring out its cost of equity using the risk-free rate of 4.45%, beta of 1.31, and the market return rate of 7.77%.

Unlike in the past, we no longer expect a double-digit growth rate of Amazon’s sales in the following years in the new model. We now expect only a single-digit growth rate in FY25, which is close to the overall expectations, after which we forecast a gradual decrease in the growth rate as a result of a worsening macro environment. Even if there’s a normalization of Sino-American relations, there’s nevertheless a decent chance that some basic tariff rate will remain in place in the end and will likely hurt Amazon’s performance.

We now also expect a gradual decrease of the EBIT margin as costs will more than likely rise and affect Amazon’s bottom-line performance in the upcoming quarters and years. After all, the operating income guidance for Q2 was already below expectations. As for the tax rate, we decreased it from 21% in the last model to 15% in this model as there’s a possibility that Amazon will benefit from the potential tax reform that Congress might pass later this year.

The assumptions for the D&A along with the change in NWC remained similar to the previous model. The CapEx assumptions for FY25 are much higher than in the previous year but also similar to the management’s earlier expectations. Considering that in Q1, the CapEx was $24.3 billion, it’s more than possible that the annual capital spending could reach $100 billion this year. The higher spending is primarily associated with the need to expand Amazon’s AI capabilities and scale its AI infrastructure. After FY25, we expect a normalization of CapEx.

Under those assumptions, the new model shows that Amazon’s enterprise value is $1.19 trillion, its equity value is $1.13 trillion, and its intrinsic value is $104.90 per share. This is a lower price target in comparison to our previous model primarily because of lower expectations for the sales and EBIT growth rate and because of higher expectations for the CapEx, which were somewhat offset by the lower tax rate.

Final Thoughts

Amazon’s exposure to China is its weakness in the current environment. While the tariffs recently have been decreased, they’re unlikely to be fully eliminated either by Washington or Beijing anytime soon. As such, we believe that Amazon’s stock has more room to fall and continue to rate it as a SELL.

data.title: Canadian Imperial Bank: Customer-Focused Strategy Is Driving Growth
data.description:
I rate Canadian Imperial Bank of Commerce (NYSE:CM) (CIBC) a Buy, for growth and income investors who are looking for long term buy and hold investments. CM is a high-quality financial institution involved in personal and business banking, commercial banking, wealth management and capital markets. It serves Canada primarily (70%), extending into the United States and Europe. The most important factors that influence my opinion of the stock are:

  • Earnings beat over the past 4 quarters, Q2 reports May 29th.
  • Consistently growing quarterly dividend with 15-year CAGR of 5.5%
  • Currently pays a 4.38% dividend yield, goes ex-dividend around 6/28.
  • Committed to share buybacks with systematic purchases (over $1B since F18)
  • Growth opportunity by expanding its presence in the U.S. and Europe
  • Well defined strategy positioning the company to succeed long term.

Seeking Alpha Quant rates the stock a Buy 4.41 (as of 5/13/25), which supports my rating.

Solid Canadian Bank Connecting to Customers

Headquartered in Toronto, Canada, Canadian Imperial Bank of Commerce is a diversified financial institution, provides various financial products and services to personal, business, public sector, and institutional clients in Canada, the United States, and internationally.

It offers checking, savings, agriculture, and business accounts; mortgages; business, car, education, home, and other loans; lines of credit, business lines of credit, agriculture loans; and cash management, small business financing, and overdraft protection services. The company also provides investment and insurance services; healthcare banking; credit cards; private banking, wealth planning, investment management, and estate planning and trust; and ATMs, as well as mobile, online, and global money and wire transfer services.

Overall, CM has a good reputation for managing risk. As seen in the chart below, Moody’s gives it a strong credit rating of Aa2, while S&P gives it an A+. The company generates $26.7 billion in revenue, earns a 13.8% return on equity and a 58.1% total shareholder return in the last year.

The majority of the business is done in Canada, where 71% of the revenue is generated, followed by 18% done in the U.S. and 11% everywhere else. The largest strategic business units are Canadian personal and business banking at 39%, followed by Canadian commercial and wealth at 28%.

Q1 2025 Earnings

On February 27, 2025, the company declared Q1 earnings. CIBC had a strong first quarter, achieving all metrics and financial objectives, posting growth in revenues and profits. This validates their strategy in my opinion and sets up a good vision of expected performance for the rest of the year. The financial highlights were as follows:

  • Key metrics showed positive growth in Q1, 2025
    • Total assets grew 4% to $1,082.5 billion.
    • Deposits grew 2% to $782.2 billion.
    • Loans grew 2% to $568.1 billion.
    • Common Equity Tier 1 grew 20 bps to 13.5%.
  • Achieved all Financial Objectives in Q1
    • EPS growth of 23.7%
    • ROE 15.2%
    • Dividend payout ratio 44.1%
    • Operating Leverage of 5.1%

Performance To Date

$10,000 invested in CM over the past 10 years, including dividends, would double an investor's money, growing to $26,100. As seen in the chart below, the bank seemed to really break away after Covid in 2020.

Valuation

Within CM’s fact sheet, the chart below shows how the company has grown the dividend from $1.74 to $3.88 over the past 15 years, resulting in a 5.5% CAGR. The growth of the dividend supports growth in the stock price. As an investor, this trend is particularly appealing as an investment paying a 4.38% dividend today is heading toward doubling that payout long term if held.

In comparison to peers as ranked by market cap, CM stands out for having the highest 5-year total return of 186.6% and for being one of the smallest in this list. The takeaway is that CM is smaller and has more room to grow vs. the larger banks.

Bank of America Corporation (BAC) stands out for being the largest amongst peers but has the smallest dividend yield at 2.4% but the highest dividend growth rate.

Royal Bank of Canada (RY) has the highest return on equity of 14.3% with a strong 5-year total return of 145.2%.

Citigroup (C) has average metrics with the lowest return on equity and the second lowest 5-year total return.

The Toronto Dominion Bank (TD) has the highest dividend yield of 4.8%. with the lowest P/Es, which makes it attractive, however it has a lower ROE and 5-year total return vs. peers.

Bank of Montreal (BMO) has the second highest dividend yield of 4.5% with a strong dividend growth rate of 7.5% and the second highest 5-year total return of 166.4%.

Overall, the Canadian banks are more attractive that the U.S. banks on multiple measures.

Outlook

Growth drivers for CIBC can be best understood by studying their strategy which, by the way, is working. Their goal is to build a modern relationship-oriented bank delivering superior customer service and top-tier shareholder returns. The bank is known for its financial health and risk management. The 4 strategic priorities are:

1. Growing mass affluent and private wealth franchise in Canada and the U.S.

2. Expanding digital-first personal banking capabilities in Canada

3. Delivering connectivity and differentiation to clients

4. Enabling, simplifying, and protecting the Bank.

The outlook for expanding in the U.S. is highly favorable. The U.S. Secretary of the Treasury recently talked about the administration’s policy as the “three-legged stool” of taxes, trade, and deregulation, which are all favorable to banking. Keeping the corporate rate lower, expanding opportunities for trade and deregulation, especially of the banking system, will create opportunities to grow.

The company reports Q2 results on May 29th.

Risks To Rating

The main risk to CM is a strong competitive set. Canadian banks have a positive reputation for banking, in my view, with fierce competitors such as The Royal Bank of Canada. The day-to-day battles are won with great customer experience online and in branches, along with attracting the right talent to the team.

From a U.S. macroeconomic perspective, recent inflation reports in the U.S. show the CPI has dropped to 2.3%, which is good news for now, and unemployment remains low at 4.3%. This means the economy is strong and the Fed will be less likely to cut rates until much later in the year if they do at all. The interest rate policy is both a risk and an opportunity for banks since they make money on the spread.

Bottom Line

I rate CM a Buy, for growth and income investors looking for a long term buy and hold investment. CM is a mostly Canadian diversified financial institution involved in personal, business, and commercial banking, wealth management and capital markets. They have developed a strong 4-point strategy that is working, in my opinion. The key to growth is winning the daily battle to attract and retain customers in Canada, and secondarily expanding into the US and Europe.

CM has performed well, beating the earnings estimates in the past 4 quarters. The company pays a consistently growing dividend with a 15-year CAGR of 5.5%. The current dividend yields 4.38%, going ex-dividend on around 6/28. Management is committed to systematic share buybacks which support the stock price.

Risks to the stock include fierce competition from other banks and financial institutions. On a macroeconomic front, inflation and unemployment are low in the U.S., so its less likely the Fed will change rates in the next few months. The U.S. administration led by the Secretary of the Treasury are creating more opportunities for banks such as CM with improvements in trade and deregulation.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Freehold Royalties Ltd. 2025 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Freehold Royalties Ltd. in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: Fox Corporation: A Strong Quarter With Most Catalysts Already Priced In
data.description:
Introduction

Fox Corporation (NASDAQ:FOX, NASDAQ:FOXA) just delivered one of its most headline-friendly quarters in years. Revenue surged 27% YoY to $4.37 billion, and adjusted EPS came in at $1.10, beating expectations by a wide margin. But while the top-line beat was impressive, the reality is more complex. Under the surface, this quarter was less about sustainable growth and more about a one-time Super Bowl windfall that masked deeper structural challenges.

Super Bowl As A Catalyst

Let’s start with the obvious. The Super Bowl was a massive driver for this quarter, bringing in over $800 million in gross ad revenue. That alone helped push advertising revenue up 65% YoY (an unheard-of jump for a mature media company). Fox called it the “most-watched telecast in U.S. history,” with 128 million viewers across platforms. In other words, this was a record-setting, margin-distorting event, and it won’t repeat next quarter.

Even without the Super Bowl, the company noted solid underlying momentum, particularly from its AVOD platform Tubi, which posted 35% YoY revenue growth. Interestingly, this was faster than last quarter's 31% growth, despite last quarter being boosted by political ads. It means that Tubi has a nice organic growth, and we can start assuming it will continue to grow.

Segment Breakdown: Strong Revenue, Squeezed Margins

Fox splits its business into two core segments: Cable Network Programming and Television. Both performed well in terms of revenue, but profitability tells a different story.

  • Television revenue jumped 40% to $2.70B, driven by the Super Bowl and Tubi. However, EBITDA collapsed from $145M to $60M, as the cost of rights and production significantly expanded. This is where the Super Bowl paradox comes in: yes, it delivered amazing revenue, but it also dragged down margins.

  • Cable Network Programming, which includes Fox News, was much steadier. Revenue grew 11% YoY to $1.64B, with advertising up 26% thanks to strong news ratings. EBITDA rose 7% to $878M, which is a solid and sustainable pace.

Fox also highlighted that affiliate fees (recurring revenue from cable and streaming distributors) grew 3% YoY, with improvements in subscriber losses for the third straight quarter. It’s a modest gain, but a noteworthy one in a sector that’s been steadily eroded by cord-cutting. Besides, it might also suggest that Tubi is pulling in new users from outside the traditional Fox TV base, which is always a good sign.

Profitability and Valuation

Financially, Fox remains a solid operator, but the picture is more nuanced than it first appears. Profitability is a relative strength of the company, with healthy cash generation and stable margins in the core cable business. Adjusted EBITDA came in at $856 million, slightly down from last year’s $891 million, but ahead of consensus. This could suggest decent operational efficiency, and it's important to note that the year-over-year decline was driven by higher Super Bowl-related costs, not weakness in the underlying business.

The company’s return on equity remains strong at nearly 17%, and its free cash flow margin, over 12%, supports continued shareholder returns through buybacks and dividends. Management also pointed out that the fourth quarter usually brings in strong cash flow, and with almost $4.8 billion in cash, Fox has plenty of room to maneuver.

Valuation-wise, the stock trades at a discount to peers. Forward P/E sits just under 11, and EV/EBITDA is around 7.4, both below sector averages. At first, it might look like an opportunity, but it also reflects market skepticism about future growth. The trailing numbers are strong, revenue is up 27% this quarter, EBIT grew nearly 29% year-over-year, and adjusted EPS rose from $1.09 to $1.10 despite Super Bowl-related expenses. Yet when you look ahead, growth expectations drop off sharply. Forward EBITDA growth is actually negative. Analysts don't see much acceleration on the horizon, and that makes the current multiple look fair.

Fox is financially strong, but the environment around it is getting more crowded and expensive. Without big one-off events like the Super Bowl or elections, future growth will depend a lot on whether Tubi can become profitable and whether Fox One actually finds demand. If not, the stock will probably stay where it is and not go higher.

What’s Driving Sentiment: Tubi and Fox One

If there’s one area where Fox is clearly outperforming its peers, it’s in ad-supported streaming. Tubi now generates over $1 billion in annual revenue and continues to gain share among cord-cutters. According to management, 65% of its users don’t subscribe to cable, a higher ratio than Roku, Pluto, or Freevee.

Then there’s Fox One, which is the company’s newly announced direct-to-consumer (D2C) platform that will launch before NFL season starts this fall. It will bundle all of Fox’s properties, like News, Sports, and Local Stations, into one subscription product, but only for cordless users. That’s a smart move to avoid cannibalizing existing Pay Television relationships. Pricing will be “in line with wholesale rates,” according to Lachlan Murdoch, meaning this won’t be a budget option. Fox is betting on brand strength and scarcity (live sports, conservative news) to drive the demand, and not a cheap price.

What struck me is that Fox is virtuously operating in this fragile environment. It expands into D2C without upsetting legacy distributors. They even said it would be “a failure” if Fox One attracted existing cable subscribers. It’s rare to hear a media CEO actively try not to steal their own audience, but that tells you how delicately the company is managing this ecosystem.

Capital Allocation and Shareholder Returns

Fox continues to be a cash machine. Free cash flow hit a record $1.9 billion this quarter, boosted by seasonal collections and strong advertising cash flows. They repurchased another $250M in stock this quarter, bringing the total buyback since 2019 to $6.4 billion, about 30% of shares outstanding. The company has $4.8B in cash on hand and minimal near-term debt pressure, so expect the remaining $650M buyback authorization to be used soon. Here are some important quotes from the Earnings Call, said by Steve Tomsic, Fox Corporation CFO:

Turning to cash flow, where we generated record quarterly free cash flow of over $1.9 billion.

In terms of capital allocation, fiscal year-to-date, we have repurchased an additional $800 million through our share buyback program. This brings the total cumulative amount repurchased to $6.4 billion or approximately 30% of our total shares outstanding since the launch of the buyback program in 2019.

We remain committed to utilizing our full buyback authorization of $7 billion. This is supported by the strength of our balance sheet, where we ended the quarter with approximately $4.8 billion in cash and $7.2 billion in debt. And since quarter-end, we repaid our $600 million debt maturity, which came due in April.

They also confirmed that Tubi investment will remain elevated in the near term, especially after leveraging the Super Bowl for marketing. Profitability for Tubi isn’t quite here yet, but they’re hinting it’s “sooner rather than later.”

Conclusion

It’s easy to look at Fox’s Q3 and be impressed, and honestly, you should be. The Super Bowl was a win on every front. Fox News continues to dominate cable ratings. And Tubi is quietly turning into one of the most important AVOD platforms in the U.S., with real momentum behind it.

But if you step back and look at the stock’s 60%+ rally over the past year, it’s clear that most of the good news is already priced in. The market has already rewarded Fox for every possible catalyst - the Super Bowl, election-year ad spending, Tubi’s growth, and even the hope that Fox One will succeed. That last one, by the way, still feels like an “if.” There’s already pushback online about its pricing could be too aggressive, and we haven’t even seen how it performs in the market yet.

In my view, this is a solid company with strong cash flow and undervalued assets, but also limited near-term upside. Unless Fox One really takes off or Tubi surprises again, the next leg higher for Fox Corporation will be hard to justify. The valuation might look cheap, but I don’t see a strong enough reason to buy it right now. For me, it’s a hold.

data.title: Navient Baby Bond: Lock In 9.6% YTM With At Least 11% Upside
data.description:
This article was first published for our subscribers, and some of the numbers may be outdated./ Today's article is a comparison between the exchange-traded and over-the-counter bonds from Navient Corporation (NASDAQ:NAVI) and the opportunities they offer us. As the capital appreciation potential discussed in the article is around 11%, it is good to add our favorite divisibility rule to the article in case you want to learn something new: A number is divisible by 11 if the difference between the sum of its digits in odd places and the sum of the digits in even places is either zero or a multiple of 11. Now, back to the article.

Company overview

Navient Corporation is a company that provides federal and private student loans. Its total assets for the first quarter of 2025 are close to $50 billion, and the current market capitalization is $1.4 billion. Its total debt is $48 billion, and the common equity is $2.6 billion. So its total debt/ total assets ratio is 1.04, and the common equity/total debt ratio is 0.05.

NAVI has 10 consecutive years of dividend payments:

The total dividend paid to NAVI's common shareholders over the past twelve months is $0.64. Its current price is $13.87, which corresponds to a dividend yield of 4.61%. This is how the dividend yield looks for the past five years:

Bonds

Navient Corporation STRUCT INSTR PARTN 15/12/43 C (JSM) is an exchange-traded debt security with a current yield of 8.45% and a current market price of around $18. It has a 9.6% yield to maturity and years to maturity close to 20, with a maturity date of 12/15/2043. Its call date was 2008, and it is callable anytime at the moment. It pays a 6% annual interest in the form of $0.375 quarterly distributions ($1.5 per annum). It is rated by Moody's with a Ba3 credit score and by S&P with a BB- score. (Quantumonline has not updated.)

The over-the-counter bond is with a current market price of around $86.87. Its maturity date is 08/01/2033, its yield is close to 7.84%, and it is rated with a Ba3 rating by Moody's.

The two securities are different, and JSM pays its distribution to holders who have the bond on the record date, while the OTC bond trades as a regular bond, where the accrued interest is added to the purchase price.

Since they are different in type, we will use the XIRR function to compare them. We do this to have the same calculation applied to the 2 bonds.

We get that the YTM (XIRR calculated) of JSM is 9.6%, with a duration of 9.61, and the XIRR of the OTC bond is around 7.9%, with a duration of 6.43. The yields are deviated, but let's equalize them. This would happen if the price of JSM increases by 14%, or to around $21. This is what we estimate JSM is really worth, based on the idea that the OTC bond is priced correctly. If we are to give some spread to JSM for having a slightly higher duration and assume its fair value YTM to be around 8.5%, we get a fair value price of around $20

Historical comparison

Let’s take a look at how the prices of the two bonds have moved over time. When market sentiment is positive, exchange-traded bonds usually have lower yields than OTC bonds. When sentiment is negative, the opposite happens. Right now, sentiment is obviously negative, which suggests it’s a good time to buy exchange-traded bonds and wait for the mean reversion.

JSM:

OTC bond:

JSM is way closer to its 2023 low, while the OTC bond is way closer to its highs. This would have been normal based on their duration, but when we add to this the yield spread, JSM looks like the obvious "buy", both fundamentally and technically speaking. We believe that there is a good chance that they will switch places at some point in the future.

In this situation, we have two options:

  1. Direct pair trade: going long with JSM, and shorting the OTC bond.
  2. Long only: We buy a JSM exchange-traded bond and hold it until the yields equalize.

Conclusion

JSM looks undervalued compared to the OTC bond, even though both are from the same company and have similar credit ratings. JSM offers a higher yield and better return potential based on the XIRR calculation. If the market continues to favor exchange-traded bonds (its usual behavior), JSM's price could rise by around 14% to match the value of the OTC bond.

data.title: Cardiff Oncology: Buying The First-Line Onvansertib Strategy
data.description:
Finding candidate tickers to add to one of my portfolios in the Compounding Healthcare Seeking Alpha Investing Group is not difficult because it is such a target-rich environment. However, narrowing down that list to a high-priority healthcare watch list of ten to twenty tickers long can be tedious. That high-priority list is mostly filled with small-cap drug or healthcare tech companies, which are almost always in a state of flux due to volatile finances and/or pipeline developments. So, the tickers on my high-priority list are being swapped in and out regularly as their conditions change. Cardiff Oncology (NASDAQ:CRDF) has been on and off that high-priority list several times, but the conditions have prevented it from finding a spot in one of my portfolios. Now, CRDF is checking several boxes to be included in my “Bio Boom” speculative portfolio as it enters a crucial stage of development with a healthy cash position. Cardiff appears to be ready to make their mark as a potential disruptor following an interesting pivot to put onvansertib into the first-line for RAS-mutated metastatic colorectal cancer (mCRC). It is possible that onvansertib could generate over $1B in global peak sales within a few years of launch, considering the unmet need in the first-line RAS mCRC population. I am on the lookout for an opportunity to start a position in CRDF as we head into the second half of 2025.

I intend to provide a brief background on onvansertib, and provide my views on the company’s decision to take on the first-line setting. Then, I will take a look at the commercial prospects of the first-line setting and how that impacts the company’s long-term outlook. In addition, I will highlight a few notable downside risks that investors should consider when managing their CRDF position. Finally, I take a look at the Daily Chart to see if I can identify an opportunity to initiate a starter position.

Background on Onvansertib

Cardiff’s onvansertib has the potential to be a first-in-class, oral, polo-like kinase 1 (PLK1) inhibitor, which attempts to regulate tumor cell mitosis. I must highlight that high levels of PLK1 expression are associated with grim outlooks for a number of tumor types. Unfortunately, RAS mutant CRC is about 45% of all colorectal cancers and is known for being resistant to targeted therapies.

Onvansertib has an opportunity to take advantage of the mitotic vulnerabilities in RAS cancers, and possibly improve the efficacy of standard-of-care therapies, including backbone chemo treatments such as FOLFIRI and FOLFOX. However, the potential synergy with bevacizumab (an anti-VEGF monoclonal antibody) could modulate tumor hypoxia, thus sensitizing the tumors to PLK1 inhibition with onvansertib. I must stress the significance of this interaction because bevacizumab is a component of first-line and second-line treatments for CRC.

This idea was a point of discussion at Cardiff’s Type C meeting with the FDA in June 2023 that resulted in onvansertib’s development strategy being changed to first-line RAS-mutant mCRC. This makes sense because there is some rationale behind onvansertib having a superior performance in the bev-naïve patients, so there is a greater potential for tumor control earlier in the disease course.

If successful, onvansertib could redefine the standard-of-care for RAS-mutated mCRC patients.

Pivoting To The First-Line

Cardiff’s initial efforts with onvansertib were in the second-line RAS-mutated mCRC patients who had limited alternatives. However, clinical and preclinical data have shown enhanced efficacy of onvansertib in bev-naïve patients. Data presented at AACR 2024 showed significantly higher ORR and prolonged PFS in bev-naïve patients treated with onvansertib + FOLFIRI/bev matched to bev-exposed patients. In addition, the randomized ONSEMBLE trial showed that onvansertib + standard of care produced a 50% ORR in bev-naïve patients, while no responses were reported from standard-of-care alone. In vitro studies also showed that onvansertib interrupts the hypoxia pathway via HIF1α regulation, which supports the mechanistic synergy with bevacizumab. Together, these data have helped validate the company’s decision to direct onvansertib at the first-line setting.

The initial results from the CRDF-004 study were promising, with superior overall response rates (ORR) and progression-free survival (PFS) to the standard-of-care alone (Control).

Looking at the figure above, we can see that the company has two doses (20mg & 30mg), with the larger dose producing a better ORR. So, the correlation between the dosage size and the scale of effect suggests that onvansertib is the difference maker, and not the standard-of-care.

The Phase II portion of the CRDF-004 trial has already hit their enrollment goal at 41 clinical sites in the United States. The company expects an update on this study in the first half of this year, so we should get more data in the coming weeks.

However, that readout is not expected to have a mature PFS in it, so we will have to wait for another readout, possibly towards the end of the year, before we have some of the key data needed to forecast their Phase III study.

Based on enrollment being completed in April, I would assume that they could be ready in Q4, which, if positive, should provide enough insight to determine the optimal dose to be used in their CRDF-005 Phase III registrational trial.

Projecting when Cardiff can start and finish their Phase III is a bit murky, but I think it is possible the company can get alignment with the FDA in a Type B or pre-IND meeting by Q1 of next year, which could allow CRDF-005 to realistically start by Q2 2026. That being said, if they have to wait for full CRDF-004 PFS maturity, we might be looking at a Q3 or Q4 start.

If Cardiff is able to initiate their CRDF-005 Phase III trial in Q2 of next year, and is able to execute prompt site activation and enrollment, patient recruitment could be accomplished by Q1 of 2027. Assuming Cardiff can secure almost every potential FDA designation (Accelerated Approval, Breakthrough Therapy, Priority Review), my rough projection puts the earliest possible approval that could realistically occur by mid-2028, with a commercial launch in late 2028 or early 2029.

Thankfully, the company has already cited Pfizer’s (PFE) BREAKWATER trial as an example of rapid approval for a similar drug. Pfizer was able to get accelerated approval using ORR in a subset of patients from an interim time point and later reached a statistically significant improvement in PFS, which they used as their endpoint for full approval for encorafenib. Notably, the regulatory pathway used by Pfizer to pursue accelerating full approval is the same as Cardiff’s registrational trial plans that have been worked out with the FDA. Pfizer’s trial commenced in 2021 and completed enrollment in 2022, which led to approval in 2024. So, I think my rudimentary 2028 projection is appropriate.

Market Opportunity

The RAS-mutant mCRC market appears to be a substantial opportunity with ~153K annual CRC diagnoses in the U.S., and roughly 22–25% are metastatic cases. Digging a bit deeper, it looks as if an estimated 45%-56% of mCRC cases are RAS mutations, so approximately 27K to 31K RAS-mutant mCRC patients are being diagnosed. I am going to go ahead and assume that 70%–80% of these patients are eligible for systemic therapy, so we could say the U.S. addressable market is 20K-25K patients per year.

With targeted therapies often priced at $10K–$15K monthly and treatment durations of 6–8 months (no confirmation on onvansertib), the U.S. market for onvansertib could be $1.2B-$2.5B per year. Globally, with ~1.9M new CRC cases annually and similar RAS mutation rates, the market opportunity could be around $8B per year.

Admittedly, determining a peak sales projection would be imprecise, but the exercise would at least be elevating. So, let’s assume onvansertib’s pricing is and treatment duration is similar to what I stated above ($10K–$15K per month over 6–8 months), and we will give it a 20%–50% U.S. market penetration. Those numbers should get you an adjusted peak sales range from $240M to $1.5B for the United States. On a global scale, assuming onvansertib captures 15%-20% of the RAS-mutant mCRC, peak global sales could reasonably hit $1.2B to $2B per year. Keep in mind that would be for the mCRC indication… if you add in metastatic pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, small cell lung cancer, and triple negative breast cancer, onvansertib's adjusted peak revenue potential could be between $1.5B and $10.4B.

Again, this is an imprecise calculation using some industry averages and current market statistics, so the numbers are not to be seen as a passage in the Holy Writ. However, the high-end numbers do illustrate how a strong clinical profile and commercial execution could allow onvansertib to achieve blockbuster status in short order.

I will point out that at least one Street analyst has a similar outlook for Cardiff, with onvansertib achieving breakneck growth and achieving blockbuster status with a few years of a potential commercial launch.

That analyst has Cardiff’s 2033 revenue estimate at $2B, which would be a 0.09x forward price-to-sales. The industry’s average price-to-sales is about 4x-5x, so CRDF could be valued at roughly $120 to $150 per share. Considering CRDF is trading under $3 per share, I think it is safe to say that the ticker is undervalued for its projected revenue. Certainly, these are just estimates, and the company will likely have to perform some dilution in the coming years, so we can’t expect CRDF to be trading over $100 per share in the next 10 years, but again, these estimates do help illustrate the tickers upside potential if onvansertib is a success.

Risks to Consider

Despite onvansertib’s clinical and commercial prospects, investors need to accept that CRDF has several risks that could impact the ticker’s performance in the coming years. First, I must point out that onvansertib still needs to prove it can make it through the FDA, so any clinical or regulatory hurdles could be a major setback in the company’s development. My biggest clinical concern is the heterogeneity of RAS mutations with different KRAS and NRAS variants, which could dull the efficacy, and allow the tumor to employ resistance mechanisms

From a regulatory standpoint, I am concerned about the potential change in how the FDA will consider clinical trial design and requirements for approval. The company is optimistic about their pathway to approval based on Pfizer’s similar path; however, I must point out that there have been some changes at the FDA, which have impacted other companies looking for approval. I recently covered ImmunityBio (IBRX) who received a refusal-to-file letter for their sBLA despite having an agreement with the FDA over their trial design. The fear is that ImmunityBio will have to perform another trial to appease the FDA. Indeed, the circumstances for Cardiff are different, but it is possible the FDA is going to take a harder stance on clinical trial designs and endpoints going forward. So, investors need to remain vigilant concerning the protocol and timelines for the registrational trial.

Market risks will also be a major concern if there are potential delays in adoption, reimbursement challenges, as well as competitive pressure from KRAS inhibitors that could have pan-RAS goals.

Of course, we have to consider the company’s finances as we get closer to a registrational trial. The company finished Q1 with roughly $80M in cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments, which they believe will last into 2027. So, it is likely the company will have to raise some cash at some point in the next two years in order to fund their Phase III trial, and possibly prepare for commercialization. Even then, the company will likely need another round of fundraising to maintain their commercial efforts, while also potentially pushing forward with one of their pipeline programs to expand onvansertib’s reach into other tumor types. So, investors need to accept that there is a strong likelihood the company might have to perform some dilutive funding in the coming years.

Despite these risks, I am giving CRDF a conviction level of 3 out of 4, and the ticker will be a prime candidate for the Compounding Healthcare “Bio Boom” speculative portfolio.

Finding a Buy

With a data update expected in the coming days or weeks, I am looking to establish a starter position in the immediate term. Looking at the CRDF Daily Chart, we can see that the share price has returned to summer-2024 levels, which provided a nice volume shelf for support.

We can see a combination of a bullish divergence followed by a high-low that bodes well for a potential reversal setup. However, I am going to wait for the share price to break and hold above the anchored-VWAP from the April high before clicking the buy button.

Once I have established a position, I will wait for the data readout before making my next move. If the data is positive, I will consider adding to the position after the volume drops, and I can identify the market’s plan for CRDF over the remainder of 2025.

Long-term, I am looking to trade CRDF to generate profit, while building a “house money” core position to be held for a long-term investment.
